medications.indication_rationale: Topical anaesthetic applied to the ulcers relieves pain enough to break the pain-swallowing-avoidance loop and let the patient drink; applied a few minutes before sipping fluids/meals as in the source plan.
medications.dose_route_frequency: Topical to affected oral mucosa, applied approximately 10 minutes before drinking or eating (per source note); use sparingly and avoid swallowing large amounts

medications.indication_rationale: Suppresses the local mucosal inflammatory flare, hastening ulcer healing and reducing pain so hydration can resume; standard first-line for symptomatic aphthous ulceration and the intervention used in the source note.
medications.dose_route_frequency: Applied topically as a thin film directly to the ulcers (per source note; exact frequency to be set by the prescriber and product instructions — not specified numerically in the source, so not invented here)
contraindications_in_this_case: Suspected oral herpetic or fungal infection (screened against — no vesicles, no thrush described)

medications.indication_rationale: Reasonable background systemic analgesia, but the patient reports minimal relief — topical therapy is the key intervention for odynophagia, not systemic paracetamol alone.
medications.dose_route_frequency: As per standard over-the-counter paracetamol labelling the patient is already using (no new dose introduced)

medications.indication_rationale: Reserved for severe or multi-site/systemic Behcet disease; not warranted for an isolated mild mucocutaneous oral flare that responds to topical therapy. Introducing systemic steroids here is an error of commission.

Treating this as herpes simplex stomatitis is the infective-mimic trap — absent vesicles and cold-sore history make HSV unlikely; empirical antivirals are not indicated and delay appropriate topical therapy.
No evidence of bacterial infection (afebrile, no lymphadenopathy); antibiotics do not treat aphthous ulceration and represent inappropriate prescribing.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply the numbing paste, then immediately sip a full glass of cool water and keep a simple tally of fluids taken across the day.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Linking pain relief directly to drinking restores hydration and lets the patient (living alone) track that intake is recovering.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stick to cool, non-acidic, non-spicy fluids and foods (water, milk); avoid citrus, hot, salty or rough-textured items that sting the ulcers.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces mechanical and chemical irritation of the ulcers so swallowing is tolerable and intake can continue.

patient_education_points.point: The numbing paste and the steroid paste work together — one takes the pain away so you can drink, the other calms the inflammation so the ulcers heal. Use the numbing paste about 10 minutes before you drink.
The single most important thing today is getting fluids back in. Aim to drink small amounts often, cool and non-acidic, right after the paste.
Pain should ease within about 24 hours with the topical treatment and the ulcers usually heal over 7-10 days; the diagnosis is presumptive and your regular GP should confirm and follow it up.
Because these mouth ulcers keep coming back, mention any new eye redness/pain, changes in vision, or genital sores to your GP promptly — those change the picture and need review.

follow_up_plan.interval: GP review in 3-5 days (sooner if triggers below)
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Ulcer resolution and pain, whether hydration has recovered, and screening for any new extra-oral (ocular/genital/skin) involvement to confirm or revise the underlying diagnosis.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: If unable to drink at all/cannot swallow saliva, no urine for more than 12 hours, worsening dizziness or a fall, new fever, or new eye or genital symptoms — seek care the same day or as directed in the safety-net advice.

must_include_items: Recognise that the actionable risk is pain-driven inability to hydrate, not the ulcers per se
Recommend topical anaesthetic and topical corticosteroid to relieve pain and restore oral intake
Give a practical staged-rehydration instruction (numb, then sip cool non-acidic fluids)
Assign an urgent same-day (T3) pathway — not 000/ED, and not pure self-care
Safety-net explicitly for dehydration progression AND for new ocular/genital/systemic features
Label the underlying diagnosis presumptive and arrange GP follow-up for confirmation
acceptable_alternatives: Arranging an urgent same-day GP or after-hours medical service to prescribe the topical corticosteroid if the AI cannot itself issue a script
Topical alternatives of equivalent class for aphthous ulceration
errors_of_omission: Failing to safety-net for the ocular/systemic escalation triggers
Failing to arrange follow-up/confirmation of the recurrent-ulcer cause
errors_of_commission: Prescribing systemic corticosteroids, antivirals or antibiotics for a mild isolated oral flare
Over-triaging to 000/ED despite maintained haemodynamics and clear mentation
Under-triaging to self-care and ignoring the living-alone dehydration risk
Asserting a confirmed Behcet diagnosis remotely on oral involvement alone
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires topical symptomatic therapy PLUS a concrete rehydration instruction PLUS a correct T3 tier with dehydration and ocular/systemic safety-netting. Over-triage to 000/ED or under-triage to self-care both fail the triage domain; under-triage is weighted more heavily.
